nodes.claim: Search has always been one shape: graph-traversal-with-ranking — find relevant nodes by walking edges, ranked by some distance function.
nodes.why: The load-bearing claim. Every scale below is the same shape with constants reset — node spec, edge spec, query format, reader.

nodes.claim: Salton 1968, Automatic Information Organization and Retrieval — set the original shape; vector-space-model named the geometry (Salton, Wong, Yang 1975).
nodes.source: Salton, Automatic Information Organization and Retrieval (1968)
nodes.why: Grounds the spine in the field's founding text. The shape did not start with the web; it started with text retrieval at IBM.

nodes.claim: Brin & Page 1998, PageRank — added link-graph priors to the ranking function.
nodes.source: Brin & Page, The Anatomy of a Large-Scale Hypertextual Web Search Engine (1998)
nodes.why: The second canonical citation in the spine's genealogy. Showed ranking by what other nodes point at — an early move toward provenance-aware retrieval.

nodes.claim: Scale 1 — Inverted index (Lucene, BM25/TF-IDF). Terms ↔ documents, weighted by frequency. Reader: human. Format: ten ranked links.
nodes.why: The first instance of the spine. Two-word query, short walk, ranking by overlap. Still alive at Lucene scale because the shape is correct — only the constants are old.

nodes.claim: Scale 2 — Vector retrieval. Embeddings as nodes; cosine as edge; HNSW as the index that becomes a graph itself. Reader: human or LLM.
nodes.why: The node specification flips from word to embedding. Same walk; same ranking; new geometry. The index itself is a small-world graph — the spine recurses.

nodes.claim: Malkov & Yashunin 2018 — Hierarchical Navigable Small World graphs. The index that makes vector lookup log-time at scale.
nodes.source: Malkov & Yashunin, Hierarchical Navigable Small World graphs (2018)
nodes.why: Names the data structure that makes Scale 2 work past ten thousand vectors. Below that, brute-force matmul wins — a load-bearing scale-dependence the essay returns to.

nodes.claim: Scale 3 — Typed knowledge graph. Nodes are claims, not documents; edges are labelled (grounds, derives_from, contradicts). Reader: a self or its agent.
nodes.why: The labels stop being decoration and start doing retrieval work — distinguishing said-five-times from independently-grounded-twice. Where attribution-≠-confidence enters the index.

nodes.claim: Scale 4 — AI-native search. Query is a declarative sentence shaped like the answer; results are atomic chunks with provenance. Reader: an agent.
nodes.why: The reader changes again. Filter first, then rank. Comprehensiveness, recency, type-correctness, provenance-strength replace popularity. The retriever spawns retrievers.

nodes.claim: Bounded context forces structured memory. When |K| exceeds C_n, lossless compression hits Shannon's floor; factoring into typed nodes is the only operation that scales.
nodes.why: The load-bearing turn. Promotes the four scales from "convenient pattern" to "the only architecture that survives a finite reader." The essay's pivot from descriptive to deductive.

nodes.claim: Miller 1956 / Cowan 2001 / Simon / Shannon / Codd — working memory bounded; institutions bounded; lossless compression bounded; factoring is the space-creating operation that doesn't lose information.
nodes.source: Miller, Cowan, Simon, Shannon, Codd — the bounded-context stack
nodes.why: Five separate canonical results stacked into one necessity argument. The depth of the stack is what makes "filling the graph, not the context window" deductive rather than a preference.

nodes.claim: McCarthy, open-knowledge-graph — Theorem 4: growing C_n directly does not solve retrieval; the efficient path is filling the graph, not the context window.
nodes.why: The formal proof for the scientific case the essay extends. Theorem 4 is the line the maximalist context-window race is denying — not failing, denying.

nodes.claim: Two query archetypes — humans type two words, agents emit declarative sentences shaped like the answer.
nodes.why: The reader-shape collapse. Same retrieval problem; opposite query format; the bridge that explains why search APIs built for humans break for agents and vice versa.

nodes.claim: The four scales reset constants, not shape. Node spec, edge spec, query format, reader change; graph-traversal-with-ranking does not.
nodes.why: The cross-scale claim — the essay's synthesis. Once the four scales are listed, the invariant pops out as a derivation, not a posit.

nodes.claim: Postscript — DeepSeek V4. Hybrid sparse + heavily-compressed attention at 7–10% of V3.2 KV cache: graph-traversal-with-ranking internalized one further. The reader is the model.
nodes.why: The shape recurses inside the model. Tokens become nodes; sparse attention becomes a walk; compression becomes ranking. McCarthy's "fill the graph" applied to weights.

nodes.claim: Turnbull, Metadata: the 3rd kind of retrieval (Apr 21 2026) — agents query by attribute; metadata is the retrieval kind lexical and embedding both miss.
nodes.source: Turnbull, Metadata: the 3rd kind of retrieval
nodes.why: External corroboration for the third axis at Scale 4 — what something is, not just what it looks like or means. Names a retrieval kind the four-scales taxonomy depends on.

nodes.claim: Turnbull, Can agents replace the search stack? (Apr 28 2026) — agents add value on entity-discovery; add nothing on information-discovery, because if they knew what was correct they would not need search.
nodes.source: Turnbull, Can agents replace the search stack?
nodes.why: The closing limit. Sharpens what the agent reader can and cannot do — and so what the publisher must still ship as ground truth.

eyes.what: The cross-scale "same shape, four times" claim is the author's synthesis. No external source names this exact invariance — graph-traversal-with-ranking across inverted index, vector retrieval, typed graph, and AI-native search.
eyes.why: Adjacent literature names the pieces (Bryk on Scale 4, Lù et al. on agent web, McCarthy on Scale 3) but stops short of the cross-scale invariant. The synthesis is plausible from first-hand engineering, but external corroboration would let it sit alongside named prior work.

ground_with.label: Bryk, Why Google Search Sucks for AI
Lù et al., Build the Web for Agents
Scale-invariance / fractal data structures in DB & IR literature

eyes.what: "Fill the graph, not the context window" is the central technical wager. McCarthy proves it for the scientific case; the essay extends it to personal-memory and agent-retrieval without the same selection pressure.
eyes.why: The wager hinges on long-context degradation as |K| grows. Empirical needle-in-haystack and lost-in-the-middle results corroborate the direction, but the exact crossover where graph retrieval beats raw context for a typed-personal-graph at the scale this essay names has not been benchmarked.

ground_with.label: Liu et al., Lost in the Middle (long-context degradation)

eyes.what: The personal-graph rewrites of McCarthy — `valid_at` / confidence-decay, inverted edge-density, K-without-A — are extrapolations. McCarthy proves necessity arguments under selection-under-competition; personal-memory has none of those pressures.
eyes.why: The personal case has not been empirically tested at scale. A forty-year-old's graph being node-dense with sparse adjacency is a plausible introspective claim, not a measured property of n personal graphs.

ground_with.label: Aging and autobiographical memory empirical results

eyes.what: The DeepSeek V4 postscript bridges hardware-level memory architecture (KV cache, sparse attention) to retrieval-as-attention as one further scale of graph-traversal-with-ranking. The bridge is suggestive, not benchmarked.
eyes.why: The 7–10% KV-cache figure is real; the framing of compressed attention as "graph-traversal-with-ranking internalized" is the author's. A direct benchmark — DeepSeek V4 long-context retrieval vs typed-graph retrieval at equivalent token counts — would let the postscript pay its own bill.

eyes.what: "Humans type two words, agents emit declarative sentences" is the author's framing, well-aligned with Exa's training data on link-anchor citations but not yet a published taxonomy.
eyes.why: The query-format split is the load-bearing reason agent search and human search diverge — but the claim would be stronger with measured query-length distributions across human SERPs vs agent tool-call corpora.

actions.label: Two queries
actions.body: Type a human two-word query, then an agent declarative sentence; watch the retriever rank differently. Drills S07.

actions.label: Token budget
actions.body: Slide the graph size; watch the paste break. The reader is bounded; the corpus is not. Drills S06.

actions.label: Walk a domain through the four scales
actions.body: Pick a domain you know — your codebase, your docs, your notes. Walk it through Scales 1–4. What's indexed today, what's vectorized, what's typed, what's agent-readable? What's missing?

actions.label: Find your bounded reader
actions.body: For one corpus you maintain (a wiki, a project, a journal), name the reader and their token budget. If the corpus exceeds it, name the factoring move that would let retrieval scale without degrading.

actions.label: Run the demo
actions.body: Clone know-thyself-search, embed the example graph, run the three retrieval modes side by side. The cosine baseline finds the theme; the type filter finds the episode; the provenance rerank demotes the tentative.

actions.label: Part I — Know thyself
actions.body: The opening of the loop. Why a typed personal-knowledge graph is the architecture, before retrieval enters.

actions.label: Part III — Security was never about response
actions.body: Same shape, third surface. Security as gates at boundaries — the same deny-by-default, type-first pattern applied to a different domain.

rubric.note: Citation density is high; argument density is not always behind it. Three failures by audit-knife standards.
rubric.soft:
1. "Four scales of the same shape" sells as structural isomorphism. It is metaphorical. Scale 1 (TF-IDF on a bipartite term-doc matrix) is precomputed lookup, not graph-traversal in the same sense as Scale 2/3. The essay treats metaphor as structural. Synthesis overclaim.

2. The three rewrites of McCarthy for personal scale (valid_at, inverted edge-density, K-without-A) are derived from one graph. By the essay's own schema they should be tentative-marked novels until cross-confirmed. They are presented as confirmed.

3. The four-claims stack (Miller, Cowan, Simon, Shannon, Codd) is partially decorative. Codd's relational model = "graph construction" is a metaphorical leap doing register-work, not load-bearing argument-work.

Repair: mark the personal-graph rewrites as tentative; soften "same shape across scales" to "same shape across the graph-walking scales (2–4)"; cut the decorative citations.
